title: Juniper JunOS Port Down - Check Physical Layer
description: Port is operationally down. Verify physical cable, SFP transceiver, and port error counters.
steps: Access device CLI: ssh admin@<device_ip>
Check interface status: show interfaces <iface> extensive
Verify optical power if SFP: show interfaces diagnostics optics <iface>
Check interface error counters: show interfaces statistics <iface>
Verify link state: show interfaces <iface> media
Inspect cable and reseat or replace if needed
If combo port, check SFP transceiver seating
If optical power is low, clean fiber connectors
Bounce interface: set interfaces <iface> disable, commit, delete interfaces <iface> disable, commit
Confirm link is restored: show interfaces terse <iface>
reranker_text: JunOS port operationally down. Check physical layer: verify cable, reseat SFP, check optical power with show interfaces diagnostics optics, and review error counters. Bounce port if needed.
escalation: Escalate to remote hands to test cable with light meter or replace SFP.

title: Juniper JunOS Port Admin Shutdown
description: Port is administratively shut down. Verify intent and re-enable if required.
steps: Confirm shutdown was not intentional (check change log or ticket)
Access device CLI: ssh admin@<device_ip>
Check interface configuration: show configuration interfaces <iface>
Verify interface admin status: show interfaces terse <iface>
Enter configuration mode: configure
Remove disable statement: delete interfaces <iface> disable
Review changes: show | compare
Commit changes: commit
Verify interface is up: show interfaces terse <iface>
reranker_text: JunOS port administratively down. Verify shutdown intent, then re-enable by removing the 'disable' statement from the interface configuration and committing.
escalation: If port immediately shuts down again, check for port security (e.g., MAC limiting) violations.

title: Juniper JunOS Port Flapping - Investigate and Stabilize
description: Port is repeatedly transitioning between up and down states. Investigate physical layer, duplex mismatch, or connected device issues.
steps: Check interface log for flap events: show log messages | match <iface>
Check carrier transitions counter: show interfaces <iface> extensive | match Carrier
Verify speed and duplex settings: show interfaces <iface> media
Check for CRC or input errors: show interfaces statistics <iface>
If combo port or optical, check optics: show interfaces diagnostics optics <iface>
If duplex mismatch suspected, configure hardcoded speed/duplex: set interfaces <iface> speed 1g link-mode full-duplex
Replace patch cable or SFP if errors are rising
Configure interface dampening to mitigate flap impact: set interfaces <iface> dampening
Commit changes: commit
reranker_text: JunOS port flapping. Check carrier transitions and logs. Verify speed/duplex and optical power. Replace cable/SFP if errors exist. Configure interface dampening to mitigate.
escalation: If flapping persists after cable/SFP replacement, test with another end device or port.

title: Juniper JunOS High Interface Errors - Diagnose and Clear
description: Port is accumulating high numbers of CRC, input, or output errors, indicating poor signal quality or congestion.
steps: Check detailed interface statistics: show interfaces <iface> extensive
Identify specific error types (MAC statistics, input errors, CRC, framing)
Clear interface statistics to establish a baseline: clear interfaces statistics <iface>
Monitor errors over 5-10 minutes: monitor interface <iface>
If CRC/framing errors, replace cable or clean optics
If output drops, check CoS queues and buffer utilization: show interfaces queue <iface>
Verify MTU settings match end-to-end if oversized frames are seen
Check optical power levels: show interfaces diagnostics optics <iface>
Replace SFP if optical levels are degraded
reranker_text: JunOS high interface errors. Use 'show interfaces extensive' to identify error types. Clear statistics and monitor. Fix physical issues (cable/optics) for CRC errors; check QoS for output drops.
escalation: If errors persist after physical layer replacement, check end device NIC and switch port hardware.

title: Juniper JunOS Aggregated Ethernet (LAG) Down
description: An aggregated Ethernet (ae) bundle is down. Investigate LACP negotiation and member link status.
steps: Check aggregated interface status: show interfaces ae<num> terse
Check LACP status and negotiation: show lacp interfaces ae<num>
Verify member link physical status: show interfaces terse | match ae<num>
Check for LACP parameter mismatches (system ID, port priority, admin key)
Verify LACP mode (active/passive/periodic): show configuration interfaces ae<num> aggregated-ether-options lacp
Review logs for LACP state changes: show log messages | match lacp
Ensure member links are not administratively down
If members are physically down, troubleshoot physical layer of member links
Restart LACP process if hung (use with caution): restart lacp
reranker_text: JunOS Aggregated Ethernet (LAG) down. Verify member physical status and LACP negotiation using 'show lacp interfaces'. Check for configuration mismatches and ensure members are up.
escalation: Escalate if LACP negotiations fail despite correct configuration and physical link up.

title: Juniper JunOS Spanning Tree Loop Mitigation
description: RSTP/MSTP/VSTP loop detected, causing high CPU and MAC flapping. Identify and isolate the looping port.
steps: Check overall spanning tree status: show spanning-tree bridge
Identify ports receiving excessive BPDUs or topology changes: show spanning-tree statistics
Check for MAC address flapping: show log messages | match L2ALD_MAC_MOVE
Identify the root bridge and topology: show spanning-tree interface
Isolate the offending port by administratively disabling it: set interfaces <iface> disable
Commit to stop the loop: commit
Ensure edge ports are configured with edge/portfast: set protocols rstp interface <iface> edge
Enable BPDU protection on edge ports: set protocols rstp bpdu-block-on-edge
Verify network stability and CPU utilization: show system processes extensive
reranker_text: JunOS Spanning Tree loop causing broadcast storm. Use 'show spanning-tree statistics' and MAC move logs to find the source. Disable the port to isolate. Ensure edge ports have BPDU block enabled.
escalation: If the loop spans multiple switches and cannot be quickly isolated, initiate emergency network segmentation.

title: Juniper JunOS Routing Engine CPU Overload
description: Routing Engine (RE) CPU is highly utilized, leading to sluggish CLI, management drops, or delayed protocol keepalives.
steps: Check overall RE CPU utilization: show chassis routing-engine
Identify the top CPU-consuming processes: show system processes extensive
If 'rpd' (Routing Protocol Daemon) is high, check for routing loops or massive route churn: show bgp summary
If 'mgd' (Management Daemon) is high, check for excessive SNMP polling or active CLI sessions: show system connections
If 'fxp0' or 'em0' traffic is high, investigate management plane attacks or loops
Review system logs for errors: show log messages | tail 50
Check firewall filter loopback counters to see what traffic is hitting the RE: show firewall
Restart the offending process if non-critical (e.g., restart snmp, restart web-management)
If critical process (rpd), investigate root cause before restarting
reranker_text: JunOS Routing Engine CPU high. Check 'show system processes extensive' to find the culprit (e.g., rpd, mgd). Investigate routing churn, management polling, or RE traffic. Restart non-critical daemons if stuck.
escalation: Escalate to Juniper JTAC if high CPU is caused by kernel or rpd memory leak/crash.

title: Juniper JunOS High CPU in RPD (Routing Protocol Daemon)
description: The routing protocol daemon (rpd) is consuming excessive CPU, usually due to route churn, large table transfers, or instability.
steps: Verify rpd CPU usage: show system processes extensive | match rpd
Check for routing protocol flaps (BGP, OSPF, IS-IS): show log messages | match flap
Check BGP neighbor status and route counts: show bgp summary
Look for excessive route updates/withdraws: monitor traffic interface fxp0 (or lo0) matching 'port 179'
Check task utilization inside rpd: show task memory, show task accounting
Ensure route dampening is configured for BGP: show configuration routing-options
Temporarily disable tracing/debugging if left on: deactivate routing-options traceoptions, commit
If rpd is stuck, restart gracefully (impacts routing): restart routing gracefully
Verify stabilization after restart
reranker_text: JunOS rpd process consuming high CPU. Indicates route churn or large updates. Check protocol status, task accounting, and traceoptions. Apply route dampening or restart routing gracefully if hung.
escalation: Escalate if rpd continues to peg CPU without obvious route churn, requiring core dump analysis.

title: Juniper JunOS Routing Engine Memory Exhaustion
description: Routing Engine memory is critically low, which can lead to process crashes or device reboots.
steps: Check RE memory and swap usage: show chassis routing-engine
Identify memory-heavy processes: show system processes extensive
Check routing table size if rpd is consuming memory: show route summary
Clean up old files to free disk space (sometimes confused with memory): request system storage cleanup
Check for core dumps taking up space: show system core-dumps
Restart processes with known leaks (e.g., web-management, snmp): restart web-management
If rpd is using too much memory, check for full routing tables without adequate RAM
Schedule a device reboot if memory cannot be freed: request system reboot
Consider upgrading RE hardware or JunOS version if leaks are persistent
reranker_text: JunOS RE memory exhaustion. Check 'show chassis routing-engine' and process list. Restart leaking non-critical processes. Check routing table sizes. Reboot if memory fragmentation is severe.
escalation: Escalate if memory leak suspected in core JunOS processes; JTAC support needed.

title: Juniper JunOS Management Unreachable
description: The device is unreachable via SSH, Ping, or SNMP on the management interface (fxp0, me0, or loopback).
steps: Attempt console access if remote access fails
From console, check RE CPU and memory: show chassis routing-engine
Verify management interface status (fxp0 or me0): show interfaces terse fxp0
Check routing for the management network: show route table inet.0 (or dedicated mgmt routing instance)
Verify firewall filters applied to loopback (lo0): show configuration interfaces lo0
Check firewall filter logs to see if management traffic is dropped: show firewall log
Check SSH service status: show system connections | match 22
Restart management processes if hung: restart ssh, restart web-management
Clear ARP cache if IP conflict suspected: clear arp
reranker_text: JunOS device management unreachable. Use console to check RE health, management interface (fxp0/me0) state, routing, and lo0 firewall filters. Restart SSH or web processes if needed.
escalation: Dispatch remote hands for power cycle if console is unresponsive.

title: Juniper JunOS Unexpected Reboot Investigation
description: The device has rebooted unexpectedly without user intervention. Determine the cause (panic, power loss, watchdog).
steps: Check device uptime and reboot reason: show system uptime
Look for specific restart reasons: show chassis routing-engine (look at Last reboot reason)
Check for core dumps indicating a crash: show system core-dumps
Review the message logs prior to reboot: show log messages | match 'shutdown|panic|watchdog|error'
Check chassis alarms for power or thermal issues: show chassis alarms
Verify power supply status: show chassis environment
If a core dump exists, upload to Juniper JTAC for analysis
Ensure device is running a JTAC Recommended JunOS release
reranker_text: JunOS unexpected reboot. Check 'show system uptime' and RE status for reboot reason. Check for core dumps and hardware alarms (power/thermal). Review logs prior to the event.
escalation: Escalate to JTAC with core dumps and RSI (Request Support Information) if caused by software panic.

title: Juniper JunOS BGP Session Down
description: A BGP peering session is down (Idle, Active, or Connect state). Troubleshoot reachability, configuration, and authentication.
steps: Check BGP summary for neighbor status: show bgp summary
Get detailed BGP neighbor info including last error: show bgp neighbor <peer_ip>
Verify IP reachability to the peer: ping <peer_ip> routing-instance <instance> source <source_ip>
Check BGP configuration for AS number and IP mismatches: show configuration protocols bgp
Verify if TCP port 179 is blocked by firewall filters: show log messages | match <peer_ip>
Check for authentication (MD5) mismatches: show log messages | match BGP
Enable traceoptions for the specific neighbor if issue is unclear: set protocols bgp group <group> neighbor <peer_ip> traceoptions flag all
Commit and monitor trace log: show log bgp-trace
Clear BGP session if stuck: clear bgp neighbor <peer_ip>
reranker_text: JunOS BGP session down. Verify status with 'show bgp summary' and check detailed neighbor errors. Test IP reachability, check AS/Auth configs, and verify TCP port 179 is not blocked. Use traceoptions if needed.
escalation: Escalate to ISP or remote peer administrator to verify their side of the configuration.

title: Juniper JunOS BGP Holdtimer Expired
description: BGP session flapped due to holdtimer expiration. Usually indicates path MTU, congestion, or RE CPU issues dropping keepalives.
steps: Identify the neighbor that flapped: show bgp neighbor <peer_ip>
Check logs for holdtimer messages: show log messages | match BGP | match hold
Verify RE CPU is not overloaded dropping keepalives: show chassis routing-engine
Check interface for high utilization, drops, or errors: show interfaces <iface> extensive
Verify Path MTU: ping <peer_ip> size 1472 do-not-fragment
If PMTU issue, adjust MTU or enable path-mtu-discovery in BGP
Verify control plane policing/lo0 filters are not dropping BGP packets
Consider increasing hold-time if path is known to be congested (temporary fix): set protocols bgp group <group> hold-time <time>
reranker_text: JunOS BGP holdtimer expired causing flap. Check for dropped keepalives due to RE CPU overload, interface congestion/errors, or Path MTU mismatch. Test PMTU with ping do-not-fragment.
escalation: Escalate to transport provider if congestion or drops are occurring in the transit path.

title: Juniper JunOS OSPF Neighbor Down/Stuck
description: OSPF neighbor is not reaching FULL state. Investigate MTU mismatches, area types, authentication, or network type mismatches.
steps: Check OSPF neighbor status: show ospf neighbor
If stuck in EXSTART/EXCHANGE, check for MTU mismatch: show interfaces <iface> extensive
Verify OSPF interface configuration (area, hello/dead timers, network type): show ospf interface extensive
Check for authentication mismatches: show log messages | match OSPF
Ensure router-id is unique and properly configured
If stuck in 2WAY, verify if network type is broadcast and DR/BDR election occurred
Enable OSPF traceoptions to debug packets: set protocols ospf traceoptions flag hello detail
Commit and monitor trace log: show log <trace_file>
Clear OSPF session to force renegotiation: clear ospf neighbor <peer_ip>
reranker_text: JunOS OSPF neighbor down or stuck. Check state with 'show ospf neighbor'. Stuck in EXSTART implies MTU mismatch. Stuck in INIT/2WAY implies timer, auth, or network-type issues. Use traceoptions for deep debug.
escalation: Escalate to peer administrator to confirm their OSPF interface settings and MTU.
